item.title: Why's he a trillionaire when his companies barely make money? — 70 replies — 205.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why's he a trillionaire when his companies barely make money?

Open thread • Replies: 70 • Updated: 2026-06-13 21:33 UTC

OP

>Tesla makes $3.9B/year, and the amount goes down year after year, yet has a 1.5T market cap
>SpaceX literally loses money and its claim to future profit is Grok, a cash-burning AI model that is losing to GPT and Claude
>Twitter is breakeven at best
What's going on here? Are white boomers just throwing money at him because they don't like seeing Indians, Chinese and Jews running shit in tech? This is the only explanation for the Elon Musk phenomenon I can make any sense of.

Early replies (first 3)

62361459
>>62361452
People rightly see him as a visionary for the future of humanity with the ability to actually do the stuf he foresees.

on the negative side much of what he envisions is distopian

and the first person to pioneer a technology is often NOT the person who gets rich off it

but you have to start somewhere, and he certainly does stuff other companies can't.

62361461
companies do not have to be profitable for people to speculate on them performing well in the future, and it helps when everyone said every company would go to zero but instead became some of the most valuable assets by marketcap in the world because they provided goods and services people wanted

62361471
>>62361459
>rightly
He hasn't even done anything, period.

item.title: Some 16 year old told me he had worked a full week nonstop, so he was told he had to take the next five days off. — 94 replies — 197.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Some 16 year old told me he had worked a full week nonstop, so he was told he had to take the next five days off.

Open thread • Replies: 94 • Updated: 2026-06-13 21:25 UTC

OP

Some 16 year old told me he had worked a full week nonstop, so he was told he had to take the next five days off. I told him that was good.

I told him that most people will not make it in today’s economy when $150,000 is considered middle class. He replied that $150,000 is a lot of money. I said sure, but the average American salary is around $65,000.

Then I asked him what kind of work he did, and he said he did some low level blue collar welding. I told him blue collar work is terrible. He responded that welders can make over $100,000 a year.

I told him that blue collar work is for low-IQ, uneducated people and that the adults in those jobs are losers and people who messed up their lives. I also said blue collar work is fun when you’re a kid, but the adults doing it are there because they’re losers.

It turned out that three blue collar workers had been listening to our conversation and told the kid not to listen to me because I didn’t know what I was talking about.

I then told the kid that when he grows up, he will realize people look down on blue collar workers for a reason and that blue collar adults are not the kind of people he should want to know.

The blue collar workers called me ignorant, yet, from my perspective, they seemed to have chips on their shoulders and came across as unintelligent.

Why are blue- collar workers like this? Are they aware of how the world perceives them?

I wanted to talk numbers because no way most blue collar makes over $100k. Can someone fill me in?

Early replies (first 3)

62342722
>>62342700
Fuck off

62342735
>>62342700
I like this spam, because it counters the AI brainwashing (trades good, factory good, college bad, IT arrogant & overpaid or Indians, close the borders).

62342744
>>62342735
What?
>>62342722
Fuck you.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62355284
>>62355262
The mind of a blue collar is truly pathetic.

62355301
>>62342770
I haven’t worked in 1,5 months and i can already feel myself wanting to just become passive and not really being impressed or motivated by anything (brain getting fried by dopamine and serotonine). You have to be exactly the kind of ultra degenerate basement dweller who spends all his time in front of screens if you think that not working at all is good. Humans need the struggle in order to feel alive.

62355307
>>62355301
You’re pathetic. Since a little kid, all the classmates of my have been retired.

62355313
>>62355301
> You have to be exactly the kind of ultra degenerate basement dweller who spends all his time in front of screens if you think that not working at all is good. Humans need the struggle in order to feel alive.
You’re probably just poor. People making a top 10% income from investments tend not to work.

62355322
>>62355301
>1.5 months
You don’t even have a non working lifestyle built yet.

62355326
>>62355307

62355338

On one hand, /biz/ loves to larp as rich and successful and on the other hand, loves to larp as muh productive and I don’t like doing nothing.
Fucking jokes.
I hear people say what you say all the time. Yet look at retired people, I don’t see you saying shit to them. Yet when it is someone young and not working, suddenly they are “unproductive.” Even though these people are clearly rich.

62355342

>>62355322

Growing up, my dad retired when I was like 5. By 10, the parents of all my school friends were retired long ago.

You cannot imagine life without work yet kids that grew up like me were surrounded by adults that don’t work. It is the normal. People like me just carry on the lifestyle.
People from successful families don’t break their back.

OP

I got a 20% raise yesterday. $120 --> $144k (plus $7k-$10k annual bonuses). I also just paid off my car which frees up $500/month. What should I do with the extra income?

Right now I basically break even when factoring in mortgage, gas/food, insurance, preschool costs, and 5% contribution to retirement (with employer match). I live in a moderately HCOL area, for context. I'm looking at an extra $1800 now per month post-tax. I'm thinking of increasing retirement contributions, put a couple hundred extra a month toward mortgage principal (5.625% interest rate), putting a little into kids college funds, and saving up what remains in HYSA for emergencies and home projects. Anything else I'm missing?

Early replies (first 3)

62363658
not your personal blog, faggot

62363663
go back you wagie normie niggerfaggot

62363668

62363673
You need to max your retirement BEFORE accounting for housing. 401k, roth, and 1k into the market in a brokerage monthly. If this is not possible move to a location that makes this possible

>>62363668

Thats when you roth max...or i you have balls margin max in a brokerage, but macro wise now is not the time to do that+roth gives you the option to pull out contributions if needed. Not enough info given to make a call on a lot of advice, but paying down <7% mortgage isnt wise in any case. Maybe if youre close to getting out of pmi range or dont want to insure the house because you can fix it yourself cheaper tjan insurance costs over time.

Maybe I'll contribute an extra $465/month to make it an even $20/year. My wife and I have approx. $130k in retirement already (I'm 32, she's 30).

She will hopefully be returning to work in a couple years which will help. Anyways I apologize for the blogposting.

PMI is if you have less than 20% equity in your home it is extra completely separate from home insurance due to being higher risk brokie.

>>62363716

I personally max my 401k and have been lucky taking out loans when the market peaks then reinvest in my brokerage after a dip (got one right before the yen carry dump and tariff bullshit). I can grab the tax deduction and still keep the brokerage up, but I wont pretend it wasnt a little luck. If youre only in the 10/12% bracket thats not as much bang for the risk. 62364118

62364143
>>62363657
If you don't have an emergency fund, prioritize that. 3-6 months, but I'd aim closer to 6 with kids. Also, what a lot of people don't consider is building additional dedicated savings funds for known irregular expenses. (For example, you know you will eventually need a new roof, so start putting a little away every month now instead of needing to take out a loan in the future) You could save for a new car, computer, mattress, air conditioning unit, etc. this way. Think of expensive things you own that will eventually need to be replaced and start saving a little each month now for them so that down the line you aren't having to pay some interest to some jew to borrow his money in a lump sum.

The rest of your plan sounds good. Congrats on the raise!

62364145
>>62363674
Can you recast your mortgage? Given that your mortgage interest is greater than the risk-free rate, that would be a reasonable move. But otherwise, you should never put more into your mortgage payments than necessary because it affords you no benefit. If nothing else, you could just save the extra money in an emergency fund, to pay the mortgage in a later month if you lose your source of income.

62364224
>>62364061

We were also moving around a lot the last four years to get to my current long-term career so she didn't exactly have the opportunity to career build. She will be working once the younger one is in kindergarten.
>>62364055
Is the idea that it's good to diversify between pre-tax and post-tax contributions?
>>62364143
Yea , you get it. It's why I can't fully commit to retirement funds. We need a new driveway in the next couple years, for instance. This raise makes it possible. The bugger question is what's the ideal breakdown for allocation.
>>62364145
We actually financed from 6.99% to 5.625% back in...April I think? With no points. Literally the day before Trump starting bombing Iran. Mortgage payment was supposed to go down $300/month but property taxes were raised so it only went down $200...

62364230
>>62363657
But do you have seven figures? If not, you're still lower class according to /biz/

62364245
>>62364224
>it's good to diversify between pre-tax and post-tax contributions
Yes. Withdrawing from pre-tax accounts counts as income, and you pay income tax on it. But that income also affects things like medicare payments and so forth, so you want the ability to control how much of your retirement money is income and how much is tax free (Roth). It's also good to diversify between retirement and non-retirement accounts, so set up a regular brokerage account and contribute to it as well, you'll pay capital gains taxes but those are much lower than income taxes (0% up to $98k/yr if you're married filing jointly), and that also doesn't count as income. That'll give you something you can tap into if you or your wife need to retire early, e.g. to take care of a parent or something.

62364248
>>62364224
man you have two kids while being a wagie and having a mortgage?

no one in your entire family tree could give you enough money to be free?

and you think you were good enough to reproduce?

62364257
>>62364224
>what's the ideal breakdown for allocation.
There is no universal ideal, that's something that is specific to your situation. I've got about 60% of my money in pre-tax retirement accounts and I wish it were much lower. Unfortunately I've been ineligible to contribute to a Roth for most of my career, so the other 40% is in a couple of brokerage accounts that I'll have to pay capital gains taxes to access. If I were to do it again I'd only contribute enough to my 401k to get the employer match, then put the rest into an S&P 500 index fund in a brokerage account. If you can contribute to a Roth, then I'd split my non-401k contributions evenly between the Roth and brokerage funds. The 0% tax on Roth accounts is no joke, but not being able to tap into them until you're 59 1/5 is also no joke, meanwhile the relatively low capital gains tax rate takes a lot of the sting out of the brokerage accounts.

>>62364248

I make $144k/year and work essentially my own hours with no boss. I have to be at work approx. 5 hours/week in the fall and spring (I'll let you guess my job) but that's it. So technically I'm a wagie but I never feel like it. Never have to ask time off for anything.

Look at the massive retail mania.Brokers are literally bending over backward to push high-risk equity onto everyday people.

Fidelity casually drops its early SpaceX asset requirement by 99.996%.Went from a $500,000 balance requirement down to a measly $2,000 just so anyone can buy in.

Everyday normies who have never read a balance sheet are getting allocations.SpaceX targets a massive 20% to 30% retail allocation just to feed the public frenzy.

Valuation relies on unproven sci-fi assumptions like building AI data centers in outer space.

Nintendo is going to continue doing well, but it was overpriced before.
Videogames are not a dying industry, they are just no longer a growth industry. The reason gaming used to seem like an amazing investment oportunity until recently was that there were way more people going into the hobby than going out and the first big generations of gamers were hitting their mid/late 30s when earnings start peaking. It didn't matter whether game companies were good or not because the gaming pie was growing so fast that their slice would grow too.
Now that gaming is as normiefied as it gets and there are adult gamers of all ages, the pie is not growing anymore and companies have to actually be good businesses if they want to survive and grow. That's why companies that were already known for a decade to be shit like Ubisoft seem to be imploding all of a sudden.

>They are getting cooked by real handhelds now and I’m going to be buying another handheld isn’t a switch for my son.
Such as? The steam deck is now overpriced and other handheld PCs are even more expensive for similar offerings. So you're buying your son a system that's at least 2-3 more expensive than Nintendo's gaming system to play kid games that are available on every platform and even phones.... kek what a tard

62360629
>>62348278
>They are getting cooked by real handhelds now and I’m going to be buying another handheld isn’t a switch for my son
Making money out of handheld consoles depends on just 2 factors: How cheap can you make them and how much money can you get from software sales on that console.
Nintendo are the only handheld manufacturer with enough sales volume to get the best manufacturing prices. They are able to even commision custom silicon like Apple does with their phones to optimize things further, something that is completely out of reach of smaller players.
Nintendo also gets a ton of money from game sales and subscriptions as they limit things like the switch internet unless you pay and own game franchises that only release on their hardware.
Meanwhile their competitors need to spend more money to make an equivalent product and the only one that actually get revenue from software sales is Valve.
There's always going to be more powerful handhelds than whatever Nintendo makes as they don't design their stuff for performance, but there's not going to be a single handheld product that is as effective at generating net income as Nintendo's until someone starts getting closer to their sales volume.
Whenever Nintendo doesn't offer the best performance per dollar ratio, it is because they are selling their hardware at a premium due to how strong their brand is, not because they are incapable of making money at a lower pricepoint. Their competitors meanwhile are always running razor thin margins and any major impact to manufacturing costs needs an immediate readjustment to the sales price, as exemplified by the huge price bump the Deck got this month.

TL;DR: Nintendo doesn't make shit deals out of their products because they are incapable of offering something better, but because they have such an advantageous position that they don't need to. If someone ever gets close enough to force them to compete on prices/performance more, they will.
